items.q: What is the difference between weather and climate?
items.correct: Weather is short-term atmospheric conditions at a specific time and place; climate is the long-term (typically 30-year) average of weather patterns in a region.
items.misconception: Weather happens outside and climate happens globally.
items.random_wrong: Weather refers to rain and climate refers to temperature.
items.why: Students treat them as different phenomena rather than different timescales. The key distinction is time — climate is statistical, weather is instantaneous.

items.q: Why can a cold winter NOT disprove climate change?
items.correct: Climate is the long-term statistical average; a single cold winter is a weather event. Climate change is the trend in averages and extremes over decades, not the absence of cold days.
items.misconception: A really cold winter proves global warming is not happening.
items.random_wrong: Climate change only happens in summer.
items.why: This is one of the most common public misconceptions. Climate is the slope of the trend over 30+ years; individual weather events are scatter around that line — both can be cold.

items.q: What is the enhanced greenhouse effect?
items.correct: The strengthening of the natural greenhouse effect by human activities (mainly burning fossil fuels) which add CO2 and other gases to the atmosphere.
items.misconception: The greenhouse effect is entirely man-made and harmful.
items.random_wrong: It is the hole in the ozone layer letting in more sun.
items.why: The natural greenhouse effect is essential for life — without it Earth would be -18 degrees C. The problem is enhancement. Students also confuse it with the (separate) ozone hole.

items.q: What did the UK record for the first time during the July 2022 heatwave?
items.why: It's A temperature of 40°C or above. “Hottest summer overall” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is an example of climate change MITIGATION (not adaptation)?
items.why: It's Switching to renewable energy. “Building higher flood walls” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately how much have global average temperatures risen since 1880 (degrees C)?
items.why: Exact answer: 1.1. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
